hardware.hapticProfile: Slow squeezing pressure, sharp release, long pause.

hero.primaryPattern: Chest tightness can be cardiac, respiratory, reflux-related, musculoskeletal, anxiety-related, or serious; chest pressure with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, lightheadedness, or arm/jaw/back pain needs emergency care.
hero.safetyPreview: Chest pressure/pain with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, faintness, arm/jaw/back radiation, blue lips, coughing blood, or severe sudden symptoms needs emergency care.

biologyPathway.title: Many Causes
biologyPathway.short: Chest tightness is not one diagnosis.
biologyPathway.detail: It can come from heart, lungs, reflux, muscles/ribs, anxiety/panic, blood clots, infection, or other causes.

biologyPathway.title: Heart Screen
biologyPathway.detail: Pressure, squeezing, exertional symptoms, radiation to arm/jaw/back, sweating, nausea, shortness of breath, or lightheadedness can signal heart attack/angina.

biologyPathway.title: Lung Screen
biologyPathway.detail: Asthma, pneumonia, bronchitis, COPD, pulmonary embolism, and other lung issues can cause tightness and shortness of breath.

biologyPathway.title: Stress/Panic
biologyPathway.detail: Anxiety and panic can cause chest tightness, hyperventilation, tingling, dizziness, and fear, but dangerous causes must be considered first.

clinical.label: Strictly Medical
clinical.title: Chest Pressure, Cardiac/Lung Red Flags & Cause Sorting

sections.heading: Stage 1 — Symptom, Not Diagnosis
body: Chest tightness is a symptom with many possible causes.
It can be harmless, uncomfortable, anxiety-related, or life-threatening. Excellent design, body. Very intuitive.

sections.heading: Stage 2 — Emergency Screen
body: Seek emergency care for chest pressure/pain with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, lightheadedness/fainting, pain to arm/jaw/back, blue lips, coughing blood, severe sudden symptoms, or known heart disease.
Do not wait to see if a possible heart/lung emergency becomes more emotionally convenient.

sections.heading: Stage 3 — Common Causes
body: Possible causes include angina/heart attack, asthma, bronchitis, pneumonia, COPD, pulmonary embolism, GERD, costochondritis, muscle strain, panic/anxiety, and hyperventilation.
Treatment depends on the cause. Wild concept: chest symptoms need context.

sections.heading: Stage 4 — Symbolic Layer
body: After danger is ruled out, chest tightness may symbolically map to constriction, pressure, fear, grief, guarded emotion, or not feeling safe to expand.
The seatbelt metaphor is useful only after the crash risk is checked.

witty.label: Comedic & Witty
witty.title: The Too-Tight Seatbelt
paragraphs: Your chest is a seatbelt pulled one click too tight.
Maybe anxiety tightened it. Maybe reflux is burning behind it. Maybe a rib joint is acting dramatic. Maybe your heart or lungs are trying to get emergency customer support.
So first: red flags.
Then you can loosen the belt with breath, care, evaluation, and actual treatment.
Loosen the seatbelt safely or keep pretending chest pressure is always just stress. Your choice.

brutal.label: Brutally Honest
brutal.title: The Chest Tightness Reality Check
paragraphs: You may have chest tightness. You may also have a constriction-and-expansion pattern.
Medical first: chest pressure with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, faintness, or radiation to arm/jaw/back can be serious and needs emergency care.
Symbolically, ask what has you braced only after danger is ruled out.
Do not spiritualize chest symptoms while your heart and lungs are waving flags.
Loosen the seatbelt safely or keep pretending chest pressure is always just stress. Your choice.

influenceLayers.title: Heart/Lung Gate
paragraphs: Symbolically, the chest can map to breath, heart, grief, openness, and protection.
Medically, heart and lung red flags go first.

influenceLayers.title: Guarded Heart
paragraphs: The Guarded Heart tightens before it trusts.
Evaluation teaches whether the tightness is emotional, structural, respiratory, cardiac, or something else.

influenceLayers.title: Too-Tight Seatbelt
paragraphs: The seatbelt image captures pressure and guarded expansion.
Seatbelts save lives; overly tight ones make breathing miserable. Nuance, the thing humans keep misplacing.

influenceLayers.title: Held Breath
paragraphs: Panic can create real chest symptoms.
Real does not mean dangerous, but dangerous must be screened.

influenceLayers.title: Safe Expansion
paragraphs: The reset is red-flag screening, care by cause, and slow expansion once safe.
Emergency signs are not journal prompts.

reset.title: Seatbelt Loosening & Chest Safety Reset
steps: Check emergency signs: chest pressure/pain with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, faintness, arm/jaw/back radiation, blue lips, coughing blood, or severe sudden symptoms.
If present, seek emergency care.
If danger has been ruled out or symptoms are known/mild, sit upright with feet grounded.
Exhale longer than you inhale, such as 4 seconds in and 8 seconds out.
Think: I expand only after I know I am safe.

naturalSupport.title: Supportive Care
naturalSupport.disclaimer: Supportive care is not a cure. Chest Tightness may require medical evaluation, diagnosis, monitoring, imaging, medication, or emergency care depending on symptoms.

items.label: Medical triage
items.detail: New, severe, exertional, recurrent, or unexplained chest tightness should be evaluated.

items.label: Cause tracking
items.detail: Track exertion, meals/reflux, breathing symptoms, cough/fever, movement, anxiety/panic, and radiation.

items.label: Breathing after safety
items.detail: Slow exhale breathing may help panic/hyperventilation after emergency signs are ruled out.

items.label: Condition-specific care
items.detail: Heart, lung, reflux, musculoskeletal, and anxiety causes require different treatment.

avoid: Do not ignore chest pressure/pain with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, lightheadedness, arm/jaw/back pain, blue lips, coughing blood, severe sudden symptoms, or heart history.
Do not assume chest tightness is anxiety without screening red flags.

brutalActions: Treat the actual cause, not the nearest convenient explanation.

clinical.mechanism: Chest tightness is a symptom with many possible causes, including heart attack/angina, pulmonary embolism, asthma, bronchitis, pneumonia, COPD, GERD, costochondritis, muscle strain, panic/anxiety, hyperventilation, medication effects, and other medical causes. Red flags require urgent evaluation.
protocol: Track onset, duration, exertion, radiation to arm/jaw/back, shortness of breath, nausea, sweating, lightheadedness, palpitations, wheeze, fever, cough, reflux, movement-related pain, anxiety/panic, heart history, clot risk, and oxygen if available.
Use medical evaluation when symptoms are new, severe, persistent, progressive, recurrent, or paired with red flags.
Symbolic interpretation never replaces diagnosis, imaging, emergency care, prescribed medication, or treatment.
citations: Educational reflection only; not a substitute for medical care.

witty.wittyAdvice: Loosen the seatbelt safely or keep pretending chest pressure is always just stress. Your choice.

biologyPath.title: Grief Shield
biologyPath.description: The emotional center contracts to protect the heart from painful interactions or old grief.
biologyPath.expandedDetails: A subconscious command activates defensive chest posture, physically pulling the shoulders forward and tightening the pectoral girdle.

biologyPath.title: Apical Breathing
biologyPath.description: Your breathing pattern shifts entirely from the belly up to the collarbones.
biologyPath.expandedDetails: You stop using your diaphragm. Breathing becomes shallow and rapid, relying solely on upper rib cage expansion.

biologyPath.title: Intercostal Strain
biologyPath.description: The small muscles between your ribs undergo chronic contraction and muscle fatigue.
biologyPath.expandedDetails: The intercostals and pectoralis minor muscles are forced to act as primary movers of respiration, leading to micro-spasms and local soreness.

biologyPath.title: Oxygen Clamping
biologyPath.description: Shallow breathing alters carbon dioxide levels in the blood, triggering a brainstem alarm.
biologyPath.expandedDetails: A drop in arterial CO2 (hypocapnia) can contribute to systemic vasoconstriction and lightheadedness, which the brain interprets as an active lack of oxygen.

biologyPath.title: Somatic Suffocation
biologyPath.description: A tight, iron band wraps around your lungs, making full expansion feel impossible.
biologyPath.expandedDetails: The physical constriction feedback loop is complete, generating a secondary panic wave that further locks up respiratory muscles.

Known heart disease, high blood pressure crisis symptoms, irregular heartbeat with weakness/fainting, or severe sudden symptoms
Blue lips/skin, severe breathing trouble, coughing blood, or low oxygen
Fever with chest pain/SOB, pneumonia concern, pulmonary embolism risk, or recent surgery/immobility
New, severe, exertional, recurrent, or unexplained chest tightness
seek_immediate_care_if: Chest pressure/pain occurs with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, faintness, arm/jaw/back radiation, blue lips, coughing blood, severe sudden symptoms, or heart/lung danger signs
schedule_care_if: Chest tightness is new, recurrent, exertional, unexplained, linked with breathing/reflux/panic/movement, or affecting function
medical_safety.self_care_allowed_if: Slow breathing, posture, hydration, reflux avoidance, and relaxation may support known mild non-emergency patterns after red flags are ruled out.
medical_safety.do_not_ignore: Chest tightness can be heart, lung, clot, reflux, muscle, panic, or other medical causes.
medical_safety.disclaimer: This app is for education and self-reflection only. It does not diagnose, treat, prevent, or cure chest tightness.

critical_alerts: Chest pain, pressure, squeezing, or fullness
Pain radiating to the arm, shoulder, neck, jaw, or back
Severe shortness of breath or difficulty breathing
Fainting, dizziness, or lightheadedness
Bluish tint to lips, face, or fingernails (cyanosis)
Coughing up blood or pink-tinged sputum
seek_immediate_care_if: You experience sudden chest pain or pressure, especially if spreading to the arm, jaw, or back
You have severe shortness of breath, feel faint, or develop blue lips
A rapid, irregular, or fluttering heartbeat occurs suddenly along with sweating, nausea, or profound weakness
schedule_care_if: You experience occasional mild shortness of breath during normal daily activities
You have persistent coughing, wheezing, or blood pressure readings that are consistently elevated
You experience occasional heart palpitations or skipping beats that do not may contribute to dizziness or pain
medical_safety.self_care_allowed_if: Slow diaphragmatic breathing and relaxation techniques can help soothe mild, stress-induced chest tightness or hyperventilation.
medical_safety.do_not_ignore: Chest or breathing discomfort requires immediate medical attention to rule out life-threatening cardiac or pulmonary emergencies.
medical_safety.disclaimer: This app is for education and self-reflection only. It does not diagnose, treat, prevent, or cure disease. Seek professional medical care for diagnosis, treatment, medication decisions, or urgent symptoms.

hero.primaryPattern: Mast cell sensitization, histamine release, Wei Qi boundary weakness, and nervous-system hypervigilance may amplify allergic reactivity.
hero.safetyPreview: Severe swelling, throat tightness, wheezing, trouble breathing, fainting, blue lips, or signs of anaphylaxis require emergency care.

biologyPathway.title: Environmental Contact
biologyPathway.short: Pollen, dust, mold, or dander contacts a sensitized system.
biologyPathway.detail: Allergy symptoms often begin when pollen, dust mites, mold spores, animal dander, or other triggers contact the nose, eyes, throat, airway, or skin. The trigger may be harmless by itself, but the immune system has learned to treat it as a threat.

biologyPathway.title: IgE Recognition
biologyPathway.short: The immune system flags the trigger.
biologyPathway.detail: In allergic patterns, IgE antibodies can bind to allergens and cross-link receptors on mast cells and basophils. That immune recognition tells the body to launch a defensive response even when the substance is not truly dangerous.

biologyPathway.title: Mast Cell Degranulation
biologyPathway.detail: Mast cells release histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins, cytokines, and other inflammatory mediators. This is the chemical alarm bell behind itching, sneezing, watery eyes, swelling, mucus, and airway irritation.

biologyPathway.title: Histamine Storm
biologyPathway.short: Vessels leak, tissues swell, mucus rises.
biologyPathway.detail: Histamine increases blood-vessel permeability and irritates nerve endings. The result can be runny nose, congestion, watery eyes, itching, flushing, sneezing, throat irritation, and a body that acts like spring is an invading army.

biologyPathway.title: Nervous-System Amplification
biologyPathway.detail: Stress chemistry, sympathetic activation, and low vagal tone can make immune reactions feel louder and harder to settle. The body may become quicker to flare, slower to calm, and more likely to treat neutral contact as danger.

biologyPathway.title: Safety Escalation Check
biologyPathway.detail: Mild hay fever is one thing. Trouble breathing, throat tightness, tongue or lip swelling, fainting, confusion, blue lips, or a rapidly spreading whole-body reaction is not a journaling prompt. That is emergency territory.

sections.heading: Stage 1 — Sensitized Immune Recognition
body: Allergies and hay fever involve an immune system that has learned to identify a normally harmless substance as a threat.

Common triggers include pollen, dust mites, mold, pet dander, grasses, weeds, trees, smoke, perfumes, and environmental irritants. The trigger is not necessarily dangerous. The immune interpretation is the problem.

sections.heading: Stage 2 — IgE and Mast Cell Activation
body: IgE antibodies can bind to allergens and cross-link receptors on mast cells. Once the mast cell receives enough signal, it degranulates.
Degranulation releases histamine and other inflammatory mediators. This is the biochemical alarm behind sneezing, itching, watery eyes, swelling, congestion, and mucus.

sections.heading: Stage 3 — Histamine Storm
body: Histamine increases blood-vessel permeability, irritates sensory nerves, and increases mucus production.

The nose runs, the eyes water, the throat scratches, the skin itches, and the airway may feel irritated or tight. The body is trying to flush out an invader, even when the invader is just pollen existing like a botanical idiot.

sections.heading: Stage 4 — Stress and Vagal Tone
body: Stress does not mean allergies are imaginary. It means the nervous system and immune system talk to each other.
Low vagal tone, chronic sympathetic arousal, poor sleep, and ongoing hypervigilance can lower the threshold for immune reactivity. The allergy pathway becomes easier to trigger and harder to calm.

witty.label: Comedic & Witty
witty.title: The Retired Mall Cop at the Garden Center
paragraphs: Your immune system is now a retired, paranoid mall cop who was fired from the mall and now works as a greeter at a garden center. He has a whistle around his neck, a flashlight with dead batteries, and a binder full of suspicious persons that includes every living plant, animal, and dust mote in a three-mile radius.

Your mast cells are his trigger finger. Every time a pollen grain floats past, not attacking, just existing, just doing its botanical job of making baby plants, the mall cop screams CONTACT and hits the alarm.
Histamine floods the scene. Your nose becomes a faucet. Your eyes swell. Your throat tightens like someone filed paperwork to temporarily close the airway.
But here is the absurd part: there is no intruder. It is pollen. It has no agenda. It does not know you exist. It just wants to find another flower.

Your mast cells are not fighting an enemy. They are fighting the memory of an enemy. Somewhere in your life, your nervous system installed a security system that never got the memo that the war ended.
Now you sneeze at sunshine. You itch at air. The mall cop is standing in the gardening aisle macing a rose bush, and you are paying for the cleanup.
The garden center has a break room with a coffee mug that says WORLD'S OKAYEST DEFENDER. The microwave has been beeping for three years. No one has turned it off.

The mall cop has a schedule he wrote himself: ALERT, ALL SHIFT, EVERY SHIFT. His binder has tabs labeled POLLEN, DUST, MOLD, and UNTITLED, PROBABLY ALSO DANGEROUS. The untitled tab has four hundred pages.

The worst part? The mall cop reports directly to you. You sign his paychecks. You wrote the schedule. You gave him the binder. Every time you brace for the worst, every time you assume the other shoe will drop, every time you scan the room for threats, you train the mall cop. And the mall cop is just following orders.

brutal.label: Brutally Honest
brutal.title: The Ghost Alarm Reality Check
paragraphs: You do not have only a pollen problem. You may also have an I never felt safe problem. A something bad happened and I never processed it problem. A my nervous system is still waiting for the other shoe to drop problem.
Now your immune system is attacking air. Congratulations. You have weaponized your own survival instinct.

Every time you were startled awake as a kid, your body logged it. Every time you held your breath waiting for a door to slam, logged. Every time you walked on eggshells around someone who should have protected you, logged, encrypted, and burned into the body’s threat map.

Your allergies may not be just a random nuisance. They can become a body-level habit of treating neutral contact like danger. Grass did not hurt you. Pollen did not betray you. Dust mites did not emotionally neglect you. People did. Circumstances did. Environments did.

Because no one helped your body sound the all-clear, your immune system became the overprotective parent you never had. It attacks pollen. It attacks cat dander. It attacks dust. It attacks scented candles like lavender committed a felony.

Antihistamines can stop the sneeze. They do not necessarily stop the terror underneath the pattern. Medication matters. Use it when needed. But the deeper work is teaching the body that every contact with the world is not an invasion.

You have been training your immune system to be afraid every time you brace, catastrophize, scan, assume the worst, or say I knew it when something goes wrong. You are the drill sergeant. You are the one who keeps the soldiers on high alert.
Sound the all-clear. Tell your body: we made it out. We are not there anymore. The threat is gone.
The person who hurt you is not in the room. The situation that terrified you is over. The unsafe house is a memory. Your body may not know that yet. You have to bring it back into the present.
The present is a Tuesday in spring. The present has pollen. The pollen is not your mother. The pollen is not your father. The pollen is just pollen. It is not out to get you.

Teach your body that the war is over. Teach it with breath. Teach it with rest. Teach it with slow exhales that say I am safe. Teach it by not bracing. Teach it by not scanning. Teach it by letting yourself believe, for one second, that the world is actually okay.

The world is not perfect. Not predictable. Not always kind. But okay enough. The threat level is green. Your mast cells are still on red. Write the memo. Deliver it. Breathe it into your body until it believes you.
Armor down or stay reactive. Charming little choice, that one.

influenceLayers.title: Wei Qi Boundary Weakness
paragraphs: In Chinese medicine, the Lung governs Wei Qi, the protective boundary between the body and the outside world.

In an allergy pattern, this boundary may act confused or underpowered. The body cannot clearly tell neutral contact from threat. Pollen becomes an invader. Dust becomes a demon. Cat dander becomes a tiny biological war crime.

influenceLayers.title: Lung-Kidney Rooting
paragraphs: The Lung disperses and descends Qi. The Kidney helps root and anchor the breath.
When the system feels depleted, unrooted, or unsafe, defensive energy may float at the surface. The body becomes twitchy at the boundary, reacting quickly to anything that crosses it.

influenceLayers.title: Hypervigilant Sentinel
paragraphs: The immune system becomes the sentinel that never sleeps. It watches, scans, suspects, and fires before checking whether the target is real.
This can mirror a life pattern where resting felt dangerous and being caught off guard once meant disaster. The Safe One, the part that trusts the world enough to relax, gets exiled.

influenceLayers.title: Ghost Alarm Pattern
paragraphs: The body may not only be reacting to pollen. It may be reacting to the memory of threat: old unpredictability, emotional neglect, walking on eggshells, or never feeling protected.
The ghost is not the pollen. The ghost is the old feeling of being unsafe in your own skin.

influenceLayers.title: Wei Qi Strengthening
paragraphs: The goal is not to shame the immune system. It is trying to protect you. The goal is to teach it discrimination: this is pollen, not danger; this is air, not attack.
Use warmth, longer exhales, steady posture, and a repeated safety cue to help the body feel protected without launching a full immune military operation.

reset.title: Wei Qi Strengthening & Vagal Restoration
steps: Sit upright. Rub your palms together until warm, then place them over your upper chest.
Inhale through the nose for 4 seconds, imagining warm gold light expanding under your palms.
Exhale through the nose for 8 seconds, imagining that protective field extending a few inches beyond your skin.
On each exhale, whisper: The world is not my enemy. I am safe.
Continue for 2 minutes. Use this as support, not as a substitute for allergy medication or medical care.

brutalActions: Name the threat your body keeps expecting. Be honest. Pollen is probably not the original villain.
When symptoms flare, say out loud: I am not under attack. This is a body alarm, not proof of danger.
Stop training the mall cop. Notice bracing, scanning, and catastrophizing before the immune system turns them into marching orders.
Use medication and trigger control when needed. Then do the deeper work of teaching your body the war is over.

hardware.hapticProfile: Rapid, scattered, unpredictable bursts like a sensor array misfiring or mast cells degranulating.

clinical.mechanism: Allergies and hay fever involve IgE-mediated immune recognition, mast cell degranulation, histamine release, mucus production, itching, swelling, sneezing, watery eyes, and nervous-system amplification.
protocol: Follow prescribed allergy medication, inhaler, nasal spray, eye drop, or immunotherapy guidance from a licensed clinician.
Reduce known triggers when possible: pollen exposure, dust mites, mold, smoke, pet dander, and strong fragrances.
For mild symptoms only, use slow longer exhales and warm hands over the upper chest to support parasympathetic downshifting.
Seek urgent care for throat tightness, wheezing, facial/tongue swelling, faintness, confusion, or signs of anaphylaxis.
citations: Educational reflection only; not a substitute for allergy diagnosis, medication, or emergency care.
Severe allergic reactions can be life-threatening and require immediate medical attention.

Swelling of lips, tongue, face, throat, or around the eyes
Fainting, confusion, severe weakness, or feeling like you may pass out
Blue lips or fingernails
Rapidly spreading hives or whole-body reaction
Known severe allergy exposure or possible anaphylaxis
seek_immediate_care_if: You have signs of anaphylaxis, breathing trouble, throat tightness, facial/tongue swelling, fainting, confusion, or blue lips
Symptoms rapidly worsen or involve more than one body system, such as skin plus breathing, stomach symptoms plus dizziness, or swelling plus wheezing
You used epinephrine or were told to use it for severe allergic reactions
schedule_care_if: Allergy symptoms are frequent, worsening, seasonal, or interfering with sleep or daily function
Over-the-counter medication is not controlling symptoms
You have asthma, wheezing, recurrent sinus infections, or chronic nasal congestion
You want evaluation for allergy testing, prescription treatment, or immunotherapy
medical_safety.self_care_allowed_if: Trigger reduction, nasal rinsing with sterile/distilled or previously boiled water, showering after pollen exposure, changing pillowcases, using air filtration, and gentle calming practices may support mild, familiar allergy symptoms.

hero.primaryPattern: Asthma involves airway inflammation and narrowing; severe shortness of breath, blue lips/fingertips, trouble speaking, exhaustion, or symptoms not improving with rescue treatment needs emergency care.
hero.safetyPreview: Severe shortness of breath, blue lips/fingertips, trouble speaking, chest retractions, exhaustion, confusion, or rescue inhaler not helping needs emergency care.

biologyPathway.title: Inflamed Airways
biologyPathway.short: The airways become swollen and reactive.
biologyPathway.detail: Asthma airways can inflame, narrow, and produce mucus, making airflow harder.

biologyPathway.title: Bronchospasm
biologyPathway.detail: Triggers can make airway muscles constrict, causing wheeze, chest tightness, cough, and shortness of breath.

biologyPathway.title: Exacerbation Risk
biologyPathway.detail: Severe attacks may cause low oxygen, inability to speak, blue lips/skin, exhaustion, or need for emergency treatment.

biologyPathway.title: Action Plan
biologyPathway.detail: Controller medicines, rescue inhalers, trigger control, peak flow, biologics for some severe asthma, and written action plans guide care.

clinical.label: Strictly Medical
clinical.title: Airway Inflammation, Bronchospasm & Asthma Action Plan

sections.heading: Stage 1 — Chronic Airway Disease
body: Asthma is a chronic airway condition with inflammation, hyperreactivity, and variable airflow obstruction.
It can be allergic, non-allergic, exercise-triggered, infection-triggered, eosinophilic, or mixed. Because lungs apparently enjoy subcategories.

sections.heading: Stage 2 — Symptoms and Triggers
body: Symptoms can include wheezing, coughing, chest tightness, shortness of breath, nighttime waking, and exercise limitation.
Triggers include allergens, smoke, pollution, viral infections, cold air, exercise, stress, and irritants.

sections.heading: Stage 3 — Treatment Direction
body: Care may include rescue inhalers, inhaled corticosteroids, combination inhalers, leukotriene modifiers, trigger control, biologics for severe asthma, and an asthma action plan.
Medication adherence matters more than heroic suffering. Shocking development.

sections.heading: Stage 4 — Safety Check
body: Seek emergency care for severe shortness of breath, blue lips/fingertips, inability to speak in full sentences, chest retractions, confusion, exhaustion, or symptoms not improving after rescue treatment.
Breathing comes before journaling.

witty.label: Comedic & Witty
witty.title: The Pinched Straw
paragraphs: Your lungs are a straw and someone pinched it while demanding you sip normally.
Inflammation swells the straw, mucus clogs it, bronchospasm squeezes it, and then everyone acts surprised when air files a complaint.
The plan is not to argue with the straw.
The plan is controller care, rescue medicine, trigger control, and an emergency plan when breathing turns ugly.
Unpinch the straw medically or keep asking tight airways to run a marathon. Your choice.

Symptoms can include cough, mucus, wheezing, chest tightness, fatigue, and sometimes fever.

sections.heading: Stage 2 — Acute vs Chronic
body: Acute bronchitis is usually viral and often follows a cold or respiratory infection.
Chronic bronchitis is a COPD-related long-term productive cough pattern, often from smoking or irritant exposure. Your lungs, sadly, do keep receipts.

sections.heading: Stage 3 — Treatment Direction
body: Care may include rest, fluids, humidified air, OTC symptom relief if safe, inhalers if bronchospasm is present, avoiding smoke/irritants, and medical evaluation when needed.

Coughing is clearing. Suffocating is not.

biologyPathway.title: CFTR Gene
biologyPathway.detail: CFTR variants impair chloride and water movement, making secretions thicker and harder to clear.

biologyPathway.title: Lung Disease
biologyPathway.detail: Thick mucus impairs mucociliary clearance, creating airway obstruction, recurrent infection, inflammation, and bronchiectasis risk.

biologyPathway.title: Digestive Effects
biologyPathway.detail: Pancreatic insufficiency can reduce digestive enzyme delivery, causing malabsorption, greasy stools, vitamin deficiencies, and poor growth/weight gain.

biologyPathway.title: Modern Treatment
biologyPathway.detail: Care may include airway clearance, inhaled medications, antibiotics, pancreatic enzymes, nutrition support, CFTR modulators, mental-health support, and transplant evaluation in advanced disease.

sections.heading: Stage 1 — Genetic Condition, Not Mindset

It is not caused by emotional stuckness, stress, or failure to release. That framing can go straight into the medical trash compactor.

sections.heading: Stage 2 — Lung and Digestive Pathway

This can affect breathing, digestion, growth/weight, vitamins, sinuses, liver, fertility, and diabetes risk.

sections.heading: Stage 3 — Treatment Direction
body: CF care may include airway clearance, inhaled mucus thinners, bronchodilators, antibiotics, pancreatic enzyme replacement, high-calorie nutrition, vitamins, CFTR modulators, and specialist follow-up.
Treatment is highly individualized and belongs with a CF care team.

sections.heading: Stage 4 — Safety Check
body: Seek prompt care for fever, worsening cough or sputum, shortness of breath, chest pain, coughing blood, low oxygen, dehydration, or rapid decline.
In CF, little changes can become big problems. Biology, tragically, loves escalation.

clinical.mechanism: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic condition caused by CFTR gene variants that affect salt and water movement across cells. This leads to thick, sticky mucus in the lungs, pancreas, digestive tract, and other organs. CF can cause chronic lung infections, impaired mucociliary clearance, pancreatic insufficiency, malabsorption, poor weight gain, sinus disease, infertility in many males, liver disease, diabetes risk, and progressive lung disease. Modern CFTR modulators have transformed care for many eligible people, but CF still requires specialist management.
protocol: Track cough, sputum, fever, shortness of breath, blood in sputum, weight, appetite, stools/grease, enzyme use, CFTR modulator eligibility, airway clearance adherence, oxygen levels if used, infections, diabetes symptoms, and CF clinic follow-up.
Use emergency care when symptoms match red flags. Use medical/specialist care for diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment.
Symbolic interpretation never replaces emergency care, prescribed medication, specialist care, or treatment.

biologyPathway.title: Blocked Blood Flow
biologyPathway.detail: A coronary blockage can deprive heart muscle of oxygen, causing damage or death of tissue.

biologyPathway.title: Time Is Muscle

sections.heading: Stage 1 — Emergency First
body: A heart attack is a medical emergency.
If symptoms suggest a heart attack, call emergency services immediately and do not drive yourself. The app does not get a vote.

sections.heading: Stage 2 — What Happens
body: Blood flow to part of the heart is blocked, causing ischemia and possible death of heart muscle.
The longer the blockage persists, the more heart muscle can be damaged. Time is muscle, and muscle is not famous for regenerating on command.

sections.heading: Stage 3 — Symptoms
body: Symptoms can include chest pain/pressure, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea/vomiting, lightheadedness, fatigue, and pain spreading to arm, jaw, neck, back, or stomach.
Symptoms may be atypical or subtle, especially in women, older adults, and people with diabetes.

sections.heading: Stage 4 — Aftercare
body: Long-term care may include antiplatelet drugs, statins, beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, smoking cessation, blood pressure/cholesterol/diabetes control, exercise guidance, diet changes, and cardiac rehabilitation.
Reflection belongs after survival, not during the alarm.

clinical.mechanism: A heart attack, or myocardial infarction, occurs when blood flow to part of the heart muscle is blocked long enough to damage or kill heart tissue. It commonly involves coronary artery disease, plaque rupture, clot formation, spasm, or other coronary obstruction. Symptoms can include chest pressure/pain, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea/vomiting, lightheadedness, fatigue, and pain spreading to the arm, jaw, neck, back, or stomach. Some heart attacks have atypical or subtle symptoms, especially in women, older adults, and people with diabetes.
protocol: Track chest pressure/pain, onset, exertion, radiation, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea/vomiting, lightheadedness/fainting, fatigue, diabetes, heart history, blood pressure, cholesterol, smoking, and whether emergency services were called.

sections.heading: Stage 1 — Overbreathing
body: Hyperventilation means breathing faster or deeper than the body needs.
This can lower carbon dioxide and cause dizziness, tingling, chest tightness, shortness of breath, palpitations, and fear.

sections.heading: Stage 2 — Panic Loop
body: Panic can trigger hyperventilation, and hyperventilation symptoms can trigger more panic.
The loop is obnoxious because the body mistakes its own alarm for proof of danger. Classic nervous-system nonsense.

sections.heading: Stage 3 — Rule Out Danger
body: Hyperventilation can overlap with asthma, COPD, pulmonary embolism, heart issues, infection, pain, substances, medications, and metabolic problems.
Chest pain, fainting, confusion, blue lips, severe shortness of breath, or first/worst episode needs medical evaluation.

sections.heading: Stage 4 — Treatment Direction
body: When serious causes are ruled out, treatment may include slow breathing, CBT, panic/anxiety treatment, exposure work, grounding, medication when appropriate, and trigger planning.
Paper-bag breathing is not generally recommended because it can be dangerous when the cause is not simple anxiety.

witty.label: Comedic & Witty
witty.title: The Gas Pedal Stuck Down
paragraphs: Your breath is a car and the gas pedal is stuck to the floor.
The engine revs, carbon dioxide drops, your hands tingle, your chest feels weird, and panic shouts, see, we are dying.
Maybe it is panic. Maybe it is asthma, heart, clot, infection, or something else trying to wear the same costume.
So check red flags first, then slow the breath like a person taking the keys back from a raccoon.
Slow the gas pedal safely or keep letting panic drive the breathing. Your choice.

biologyPathway.short: Imaging, labs, biopsy, and staging guide care.
biologyPathway.detail: Cancer workup depends on the organ involved and often requires pathology confirmation.

biologyPathway.title: Treatment
biologyPathway.detail: Surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, hormone therapy, surveillance, or combinations may be used.

biologyPathway.title: Support
biologyPathway.detail: Counseling, support groups, symptom control, nutrition, sleep, and practical help matter. Cancer already brings enough paperwork from hell.

clinical.label: Strictly Medical
clinical.title: Persistent Cough, Imaging, Biopsy & Lung Cancer Screening

sections.heading: Stage 1 — Lung Signals
body: Persistent cough, coughing blood, shortness of breath, chest pain, hoarseness, and unexplained weight loss need evaluation.
The lungs should not have to send smoke signals.

sections.heading: Stage 2 — Screening
body: High-risk adults may qualify for annual low-dose CT screening.
Screening is for people at risk, not a replacement for quitting smoking or symptom evaluation.

sections.heading: Stage 3 — Diagnosis
body: Workup may include chest imaging, CT, PET, bronchoscopy, biopsy, and molecular testing.
Tumor markers can affect targeted therapy or immunotherapy options.

sections.heading: Stage 4 — Treatment
body: Treatment may include surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, or palliative/supportive care depending on type and stage.
Air matters. This should not be controversial.

witty.label: Comedic & Witty
witty.title: The Blocked Air System
paragraphs: The air system is throwing alarms, and pretending the smoke detector is just dramatic is how humans lose buildings.
The useful plan is not denial, not panic, and not internet supplement roulette.
The useful plan is evaluation, diagnosis, staging, treatment, support, and asking very specific questions until the medical fog starts behaving.
The symbolism can help with meaning. It does not get to fire the oncologist.
Check the lungs and build the plan, or keep asking breath to work around a blocked air system. Your choice.

brutal.label: Brutally Honest
brutal.title: The Medical Reality Check
paragraphs: You may have Lung Cancer or a cancer concern. You may also have a shock-and-meaning pattern.
Medical first: suspected cancer needs prompt evaluation. Diagnosed cancer needs an oncology plan.
Screening, biopsy, imaging, staging, and treatment decisions belong with qualified clinicians.
Symbolically, ask what this diagnosis forces you to face, but do not blame yourself for cellular disease.
Check the lungs and build the plan, or keep asking breath to work around a blocked air system. Your choice.

influenceLayers.title: Qi Stagnation / Blood Stasis Image
paragraphs: In TCM imagery, masses may be discussed through stagnation or stasis.
Medically, cancer is cellular disease requiring oncology care.

influenceLayers.title: The Breath Keeper
paragraphs: The symbolic layer here is breath, grief, receiving life, smoke, exposure, and fear without blame.
Meaning can support coping. It must never become blame.

influenceLayers.title: The Blocked Air System
paragraphs: The lungs are the air system, and a persistent cough or blood in the sputum is not something to file under probably fine.
The metaphor is emotional scaffolding, not a treatment plan.

influenceLayers.title: Support System
paragraphs: Cancer care is a team sport nobody asked to join.
Bring notes, questions, second opinions when needed, and a person who can remember what the doctor said while your brain becomes soup.

influenceLayers.title: Treatment Plus Meaning
paragraphs: The reset is medical action plus nervous-system support.
Both matter. One does not replace the other.

reset.title: Breath System & Care Reset
steps: Sit somewhere safe.
Place one hand on the heart and one on the affected area if comfortable.
Inhale through the nose for 4 seconds.
Exhale slowly for 8 seconds.
Think: I am not alone. I take the next medical step. I support my body without blaming it.

naturalSupport.title: Supportive Care
naturalSupport.disclaimer: Supportive care is not a cure. Lung Cancer may require medical evaluation, diagnosis, and condition-specific treatment.

items.label: Prompt symptom care
items.detail: Coughing blood, persistent cough, chest pain, shortness of breath, or weight loss needs evaluation.

items.label: Screening if high risk
items.detail: Ask about low-dose CT if age/smoking history fits screening criteria.

items.label: Smoking support
items.detail: Quitting support improves health at any stage and belongs without shame.

items.label: Molecular testing
items.detail: Ask whether tumor testing affects targeted or immune treatment options.

avoid: Do not ignore coughing blood or persistent cough.
Do not delay evaluation because you fear the answer.
Do not replace oncology care with lung detox nonsense.

brutalActions: Do not let fear, shame, or miracle-cure marketing run the treatment plan.

clinical.mechanism: Lung cancer is cancer arising in lung tissue. Warning signs can include persistent or changing cough, coughing blood, shortness of breath, chest pain, hoarseness, unexplained weight loss, recurrent pneumonia, fatigue, or wheezing. High-risk people may qualify for annual low-dose CT screening.
protocol: Track symptoms, screening history, family history, diagnosis stage if known, treatment plan, side effects, weight loss, pain, bleeding, fatigue, fever, infection signs, and urgent changes. Follow oncology guidance.
Use medical evaluation when symptoms are new, severe, progressive, persistent, recurrent, or paired with red flags.
Symbolic interpretation never replaces diagnosis, screening, emergency care, or treatment.
citations: Educational reflection only; not a substitute for medical care.

witty.wittyAdvice: Check the lungs and build the plan, or keep asking breath to work around a blocked air system. Your choice.

critical_alerts: Coughing blood, severe shortness of breath, chest pain, blue lips, or respiratory distress
Persistent or worsening cough, recurrent pneumonia, unexplained weight loss, hoarseness, or fatigue
Abnormal chest imaging, high-risk smoking history, asbestos/radon/occupational exposure concern
Fever/infection signs during treatment or severe treatment side effects
seek_immediate_care_if: Severe symptoms, bleeding, trouble breathing, neurologic deficits, severe pain, infection signs during treatment, or feeling medically unsafe occur
schedule_care_if: Symptoms persist or imaging is abnormal
High-risk screening eligibility, smoking cessation support, diagnosis, staging, or treatment planning is needed
medical_safety.self_care_allowed_if: Supportive routines, sleep, nutrition, movement as tolerated, counseling, and support groups may help alongside medical care.
medical_safety.do_not_ignore: Cancer symptoms, screening abnormalities, new lumps, unexplained weight loss, bleeding, persistent cough, neurologic symptoms, or treatment side effects need medical guidance.
medical_safety.disclaimer: This app is for education and self-reflection only. It does not diagnose, treat, prevent, or cure cancer.
